turns.content: I need some help getting my week organized. feels like im running around with my head cut off every day at the shop
I can help with that. Let's start with the basics — what does a typical week look like for you right now? What are your shop hours, and what keeps eating up your time the most?
open monday thru saturday. monday thru friday 7 to 6, saturdays 8 to 2. I got 3 guys working for me and I swear half my day is putting out fires instead of turning wrenches

That's a long week. And the "putting out fires" problem is really common for shop owners who are also the lead tech. You're trying to do two jobs at once.

A few questions: What kind of fires are we talking about? Parts ordering, customer calls, scheduling jobs, employee stuff? And do you have any time blocked off during the week that's non-negotiable — personal stuff, appointments, anything like that?

all of the above honestly. parts ordering kills me, customers calling wanting updates, trying to figure out who's working on what. thursdays poker night so I gotta close shop on time for once. other than that its just work work work

OK so Thursday you've got a hard stop — that's actually useful because it forces a deadline on the day. Let's use that same mentality for other days.

Here's what I'd suggest as a starting framework:

1. **Batch your parts ordering.** Instead of ordering throughout the day as jobs come in, set two windows — first thing in the morning and right after lunch. Check what's needed, place orders in bulk. Saves you from constant interruption.

2. **Set customer update times.** Pick two times a day where you or one of your guys calls customers with updates. Morning and around 3pm works well. This cuts down on inbound calls because people aren't wondering.

3. **Use a whiteboard or job board** if you don't already. Each bay, what's in it, who's on it, what stage it's at. Saves you from answering "what should I work on next" ten times a day.

Would any of that work with how your shop runs?
the parts ordering thing is smart. I do it like 6 times a day which is stupid when I think about it. we got a whiteboard but nobody uses it right

The whiteboard only works if it's dead simple. I'd set it up with columns: Vehicle In, In Progress, Waiting on Parts, Done. Each job gets a card or a line with the customer name and the car. When a job moves, the card moves.

Make one of your guys responsible for updating it. Give them ownership of it. If it's everybody's job it's nobody's job.

For parts ordering, try the two-window approach this week. Morning batch and afternoon batch. You'll be amazed how much time it frees up.
yeah that makes sense. I got one guy Reggie who's pretty organized, he'd probably be good for the board. what about saturdays, its a short day but it always feels wasted

Saturdays are gold if you use them right. Six hours is tight, so I'd make Saturday a "finishers only" day. No new big jobs. Use it to close out anything from the week that's 80% done, and do oil changes, tire rotations, quick stuff that turns fast money.

Also — Saturday morning is great for walk-in customers who can't come during the week. Those tend to be loyal repeat customers if you treat them right.

Don't start any job on Saturday you can't finish by 2. That way you're not dragging work into Monday.
finishers only. I like that. we always start something big saturday morning and then everyones scrambling at 1:30

Exactly, and then either you stay late or the job sits half-done over the weekend. Neither one is good.

Set the rule and stick to it. If a customer wants a big job Saturday, book them for Monday. They'll understand.

So to recap your game plan: batch parts ordering twice a day, give Reggie the whiteboard, set customer update call times, and make Saturday a finishers-only day. Try it for two weeks and see how it feels.
alright man im gonna try all this starting next week. appreciate it

Sounds good. Start with the parts batching and the Saturday rule — those two alone should make a noticeable difference. You can layer in the rest as it becomes habit.

Hope you have a good Thursday this week. Take care.
